coredata.dc:title: Recent progress in smartphone-based techniques for food safety and the detection of heavy metal ions in environmental water
coredata.dc:creator: Sivakumar, Rajamanickam
Lee, Nae Yoon
coredata.dc:description: Emerging smartphone-based point-of-care tests (POCTs) are cost-effective, precise, and easy to implement in resource-limited areas. Thus, they are considered a potential alternative to conventional diagnostic testing. This review explores food safety and the detection of metal ions in environmental water based on unprecedented smartphone technology. Specifically, we provide an overview of various methods used for target analyte detection (antibiotics, enzymes, mycotoxins, pathogens, pesticides, small molecules, and metal ions), such as colorimetric, fluorescence, microscopic imaging, and electrochemical methods. This paper performs a comprehensive review of smartphone-based POCTs developed in the last three years (2018–2020) and evaluates their relative advantages and limitations. Moreover, we discuss the imperative role of new technology in the progress of POCTs. Sensor materials (metal nanoparticles, carbon dots, quantum dots, organic substrates, etc.) and detection techniques (paper-based, later flow assay, microfluidic platform, etc.) involved in POCTs based on smartphones, and the challenges faced by these techniques, are addressed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Smartphone-based colorimetric and fluorescent methods for food safety and metal ion detection in environmental water.
A schematic diagram for detection of streptomycin using a smartphone (Lin et al., 2018).

Schematic diagram of the smartphone-based LFIA method for multiplex mycotoxin detection. (A) Preparation of the immunosensor; (B) detection of mycotoxins in the test strips; (C) qualitative test results; (D) quantitative test results (Liu et al., 2020b).
Magnetic beads and inorganic nanoflower-based sandwich immunosensor for detection of Salmonella enteritidis (Zeinhom et al., 2018a).
Schematic design of two-way LFIA for detection of acetochlor and fenpropathrin (Cheng et al., 2019).
Schematic illustration of an ESPIT-based sensor for detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions (Shen and Zhang, 2021).

(A) Picture of a smartphone imaging-based sensing platform. (B) Steps for detecting F− ions using a smartphone-based sensing platform. (C) The fluorescent images of the test system upon addition of different amounts of F− ions and (D) the ratio of R/G channel values versus F− ion concentration (Zhang et al., 2019a).
CdSe/ZnS QD-based fluorescence sensor for detecting mercury ions (Liu et al., 2019).
Detection of cadmium ions by a smartphone-based colorimetric reader system (Gan et al., 2020).

head.ce:title: Recent progress in smartphone-based techniques for food safety and the detection of heavy metal ions in environmental water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Emerging smartphone-based point-of-care tests (POCTs) are cost-effective, precise, and easy to implement in resource-limited areas. Thus, they are considered a potential alternative to conventional diagnostic testing. This review explores food safety and the detection of metal ions in environmental water based on unprecedented smartphone technology. Specifically, we provide an overview of various methods used for target analyte detection (antibiotics, enzymes, mycotoxins, pathogens, pesticides, small molecules, and metal ions), such as colorimetric, fluorescence, microscopic imaging, and electrochemical methods. This paper performs a comprehensive review of smartphone-based POCTs developed in the last three years (2018–2020) and evaluates their relative advantages and limitations. Moreover, we discuss the imperative role of new technology in the progress of POCTs. Sensor materials (metal nanoparticles, carbon dots, quantum dots, organic substrates, etc.) and detection techniques (paper-based, later flow assay, microfluidic platform, etc.) involved in POCTs based on smartphones, and the challenges faced by these techniques, are addressed.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional bioanalytical tools for quantitative analysis of various biomarkers are relatively expensive, bulky, difficult to handle, and their results are only accessible after several hours or days, which greatly constrains their application in the field of disease diagnostics (Harbeck et al., 2014; Parkinson et al., 2014). Additionally, accurate and highly consistent disease diagnosis results rely on expensive instruments and highly trained personnel. These factors cause considerable healthcare issues in developing countries, particularly in low-income areas. Therefore, affordable and portable instruments that could be alternatives for conventional equipment are in high demand for point-of-care testing (POCT). Such devices can be commercialized for molecular diagnosis and utilized in resource-limited areas (Darwish et al., 2018; Ding et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2018). The recent advancement of the lab-on-a-chip (LOC) technique has effectively involved the reduction of traditional bulky operations and integrates various techniques, such as actuators, biosensors, micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS), and microfluidics, in a miniaturized platform (Chin et al., 2012; Jung et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019b). Nevertheless, miniaturized LOCs for practical applications still require additional devices that are compact and can obtain accurate results.

Recently, new technology has been developed for healthcare systems that offer patients the ability to check their health conditions by self-testing, which avoids unnecessary visits to hospitals as well as prevents additional infections such as nosocomial infections to patients. These POCT instruments are user-friendly because they reduce the need of user intervention during operation and human error is substantially decreased. The results obtained from modern technology are reliable, provide rapid diagnosis results, and, more importantly, are easy to procure at an affordable price. Therefore, a new instrument, called smartphones have been integrated into various techniques, such as cytometric analysis (Knowlton et al., 2017), microfluidic devices (Jing et al., 2021; Shafiee et al., 2015), lateral flow assays (Liu et al., 2020b; Yu et al., 2015), and colorimetric tests (Liu et al., 2020a; Peng et al., 2020), for POCT applications. Modern smartphones are similar to mini-computers and have various features, such as a high-definition camera, touch screen, several communication possibilities (Bluetooth and Wi-Fi), light sensors, large storage memory, a rechargeable battery, and a global positioning system. Based on their features, smartphone-based bioanalytical devices have received much attention as evidenced by recently published articles, particularly in the field of food safety and environmental samples (Jain et al., 2020; Nelis et al., 2020; Rateni et al., 2017).

Food safety is a major concern that receives worldwide attention. Specifically, harmful substances in food that cause serious illness in humans, as well as directly affect the economy of food industry-related business, is a major focus. Furthermore, hazardous substances are entering food products due to the utilization of more fertilizer for high yield and even long-term storage of food products can be harmful (Jin et al., 2019). Currently, various categories of food contaminants, such as pathogens, mycotoxins, enzymes, antibiotics, pesticides, and metal ions, are leading to food poisoning (Guo et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018). Therefore, the detection of these hazardous substances in food samples is mandatory to confirm food quality. Several techniques, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, have been developed for food safety and each method has its own advantages and limitations (Malik et al., 2010; Petrarca et al., 2016; Romero-González, 2015). Recently, smartphone-based techniques have been involved in precise food safety inspection as these techniques are more convenient than other traditional methods (Jain et al., 2020; Nelis et al., 2020; Rateni et al., 2017).

Environmental samples, water resources in particular, are highly contaminated by heavy metals and metalloids, which should be investigated because these contaminates cause severe health-related problems in humans (Jain et al., 2020). In natural water sources, several types of metals are present and most of them are toxic when exceeding certain limits. The burning of fossil fuels, coal combustion residues, acid rain, sewage sludge, and agricultural activities are sources of heavy metals and metalloids that contribute to water contamination. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency and the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, the metals mercury, arsenic, cadmium, chromium, selenium, and lead are on a high priority list of harmful substances that are serious threats to the human environment (Piriya et al., 2017). In consideration of metal pollution in water, the development of reliable detection techniques is in great need. Various conventional techniques, such as HPLC, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), ion chromatography, and atomic absorption spectrophotometry, have been used for metal ion detection (Barron et al., 2006; Li et al., 2015; Lin et al., 2017; Sang et al., 2008). Recently, smartphone-based techniques have been effectively developed for the detection of metal ions and have clearly interpreted results compared with conventional methods (Jain et al., 2020; Nguyen et al., 2018; Tian et al., 2019).

Analytical techniques in the field of food, environmental science, and healthcare are well-developed in terms of technology and utilization. The unique features of these techniques have to be further integrated into a colorimetric platform for improving sensitivity and accuracy. Various factors are involved in the visual change of target analytes and additional devices, such as computers, scanners, and smartphone cameras, are necessary for the exact quantification of the color of analytes. Owing to its precision and rapidity, a smartphone-assisted visual platform shows great potential for food safety and heavy metal detection. Moreover, very few review articles based on smartphone-assisted visual methods for food safety (Nelis et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2019; Rateni et al., 2017) and metal ion detection (Jain et al., 2020) have been published thus far. This review emphasizes recent developments in food safety and metal ion detection using different sensing platforms consisting of smartphone technology (Fig. 1 ). This review primarily focuses on smartphone integrated colorimetric and fluorescent strategies used to detect antibiotics, enzymes, mycotoxins, pathogens, pesticides, small molecules, and metal ions. Finally, the current challenges and future prospects of smartphone integrated POCT systems are summarized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General techniques involved in smartphone-based applications

ce:section.ce:para: The latest smartphones have built-in dual cameras on their front and backside, which contain complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) image sensors (CIS). The color filter and microlens on a CIS pixel were deliberately utilized for colorimetric sensing (Agranov et al., 2003). Bayer mosaic is the general pattern for a color filter that contains red, green, and blue (RGB) filters with a resolution of one pixel each (Lukac et al., 2005). The RGB color filters are combined with each pixel and have a specific wavelength in the range of 600–700 nm (R), 500–600 nm (G), and 400–500 nm (B). The intensities of each color are represented on an absolute scale of 0–255 (Emmanuel et al., 2020). Other functions, including cyan, magenta, yellow, black (CMYK) and hue, saturation, and value (HSV), are also used when RGB image analysis is affected by external issues, such as color intensity and brightness (Ibraheem et al., 2012). Essentially, any color can be converted into basic RGB colors and image processing software is used to read out the colors.

In the fluorescent technique, fluorescence color is converted into digital data, similar to a colorimetry technique. This technique has been effectively used in the fields of biological and environmental science due to its high sensitivity. Ratiometric fluorescent is an essential strategy because it can realize intrinsic self-correction for signals by calculating the ratio between two fluorescence intensities of two different wavelengths. Thus, the accuracy of the target analytes is improved (Ye et al., 2020). Furthermore, a smartphone-based fluorescent device has shown comparable analytical performance as a conventional spectrofluorimeter (Yu et al., 2014).

Smartphone cameras can be enhanced further when integrated with external detachable lenses that function as a portable microscope. A key component, the CIS embedded in the smartphone, contains peripherical readout circuits and a photodiode pixel array that can convert an optical image into electronic signals (Liu et al., 2017). With the combination of an inkjet-printed lens and 3D-printed attachable accessories, smartphone-based microscopes are able to achieve one-micron spatial resolution, which has been used for multicolor fluorescence imaging (Sung et al., 2017).

In an electronic device, the variation of the electrical parameter reflects the quantity of an analyte. Advanced features of smartphones connected with electrochemical systems can remarkably simplify functions compared with conventional methods. Different types of electrochemical apparatuses are used in this technique. Therefore, smartphone-based electrochemical sensors have been classified into three categories: amperometric, potentiometric, and impedimetric. Owing to their portability, high sensitivity, and capacity to perform analytical measurements in remote areas, smartphone-based electrochemical techniques are of interest for the on-site detection of analytes (Liao et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020).

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a combination of electrochemistry and the luminescence process. In general, ECL signals are produced by various luminophores, such as ruthenium complexes, acridinium esters, luminol, and their derivatives (Hu and Xu, 2010). The luminance generated in this process is captured by a smartphone camera and further analyzed using smartphone applications (apps). High sensitivity, efficient light emission, low background signal, and rapid analysis make ECL an emerging technique in the field of food safety (Li et al., 2019b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based techniques for food safety monitoring

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are extensively used in animal husbandry and agriculture to control bacterial infections. However, nowadays antibiotic residues can exceed their maximum limit in food products, which causes severe health problems in humans. Recently, smartphone-based techniques have been involved in detecting various antibiotics, such as chloramphenicol (CAP), fluoroquinolones (FQs), quinolones (QNs), streptomycin, and tetracycline (TC), at trace levels. An immunochromatographic technique, in which a smartphone camera was used as a detector, was employed to detect CAP at the nanogram level in milk (Yang et al., 2019). In this process, gold-labeled CAP produces a red band in the test line when bovine serum albumin (BSA) is present. The smartphone camera captured the image and processed it into RGB format. More importantly, this process introduced a bar code technique and accurately detected CAP in milk with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6 ng mL−1 (Yang et al., 2019). In another method, a different technique was used for the detection of CAP wherein aptasensor and lanthanum (La3+) were utilized. Aptamer-labeled gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) aggregated in the presence of La3+, however, with the addition of CAP, the color of the solution returned to red. Because the aptamer readily forms a complex with CAP, the AuNPs are dispersed. Finally, the Touch Color app installed in the Android smartphone was used to quantify CAP and the LOD was 5.88 nM (Wu et al., 2019).

The ratiometric fluorescence sensing method has been applied for the accurate detection of FQs in milk. Gatifloxacin (GFLX) is a typical example of a FQ that could successfully initiate fluorescence color change in synthesized CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs). The color changed from yellow to blue fluorescence through a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. An ultraviolet light-emitting diode (UV-LED, 365 nm) was the energy source used to excite QD-coated paper strips in the presence of GFLX. The naked-eye observation was not able to monitor the slight variation of color; a smartphone camera was employed to capture the fluorescence image, which was converted into digital values of a RGB color channel using the Color Picker app. The established fluorescence sensor was used to detect GFLX at a concentration range of 0.8–3.6 μM (Ye et al., 2020). Li et al. have proposed a sensitive and colorimetric immunoassay microarray-based method to detect QN residues in milk. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were immobilized on the immunosensor of QNs in a microarray and silver enhancement solutions, such as silver nitrate and hydroquinone, were then added to the microarray, producing color. Interestingly, the TCs and QNs were detected simultaneously in this method. GenePix Pro 3.0 software was used to quantify the image over the microarray. The LOD of TCs and QNs in milk were 1.51 and 1.74 ng mL−1, respectively, and these results were comparable with commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit results (r-Biopharm AG, Darmstadt, Germany) (Li et al., 2017).

TC is a common antibiotic and it is used in humans and in livestock husbandry to treat infectious diseases. However, TC residue in food products has adverse effects on human health. To detect TCs, a ratiometric fluorescence approach, using g-C3N4 nanosheets combined with europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3, Eu3+), was applied. In this process, the TC can chelate with Eu3+, which enhances red emission and simultaneously quenches blue emission of the g-C3N4 nanosheets through the inner filter effect (IFE). Using a color recognizer application, the photographed image was converted into digital information (RGB values). The ratio of red and blue channels (R/B) showed a linear relationship with TC concentration in the range of 0.5–20 μM with a LOD of 0.25 μM. In real sample analysis, 5 μM of TC was injected in milk and detected at 5.21 μM with a recovery of 104.2% (Han et al., 2020). Another antibiotic, streptomycin, has been detected using the aptasensor method. Streptomycin is preferentially attached to an aptamer to produce the streptomycin-aptamer complex. However, unreacted aptamer in the complex was hybridized with complementary DNA in a dsDNA probe. Then, non-fluorescent SYBR Green I was added and green fluorescence was emitted in the presence of the dsDNA. Based on the streptomycin concentration, the fluorescence intensity of SYBR Green I varied. The Touch Color app (Wu et al., 2019) was used to readout the RGB values of the fluorescence images and its averaged gray values were calculated to determine the exact quantity of streptomycin present in the solution. The LOD of streptomycin was 94 nM and, in real sample analysis, 90 μM of streptomycin was injected in chicken and detected at 90.5 μM with a recovery of 101% (Fig. 2 ) (Lin et al., 2018).

Milk and its derivatives contain various microorganisms that commonly produce foodborne diseases when they exceed a certain limit. Pasteurization is a well-known method to destroy these pathogens. However, an incomplete pasteurization process would be unable to kill all infectious bacteria (Rankin et al., 2010). ALP is a metalloprotein naturally existing in raw milk that has a higher temperature-resistance than pathogenic bacteria. The loss of ALP activity indicates proper pasteurization and confirms the quality of milk. Yu et al. developed AuNP-based lateral flow strips to quantitatively detect ALP activity in raw milk. ALP has dephosphorylation properties, therefore, a phosphate-based immunobiosensor was developed and the color change in the lateral flow strips was quantified using MATLAB® code (Mathworks cop, USA). Using this method, ALP was detected in milk at 0.1 U/L within 10 min (Yu et al., 2015). In another method, the same dephosphorylation strategy was followed (Yu et al., 2015) with a paper-based immunobiosensor developed to detect ALP. The fabricated ALP-immunobiosensor produced a green color when the 5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl phosphate was added. Digital image colorimetry (DIC) integrated with a smartphone was employed to obtain RGB channel values from the captured images. Based on the DIC measurements, the detection limit of ALP was 0.87 U/mL (Mahato and Chandra, 2019). Other than colorimetry, chemiluminescence-based imaging was recently introduced to detect ALP. Adamantly1,2-dioxetane phosphate is a commercially available compound that was used as a chemiluminogenic substrate in this process. ImageJ software was used to convert the chemiluminescence image, acquired by a smartphone, and the LOD of ALP in milk was 4.4 mU L−1; higher sensitivity than results produced by digital camera (6.2 mU L−1) and luminometer (9.6 mU L−1) (Sevastou et al., 2020).

Food contamination has always been a primary concern worldwide because it produces various diseases in humans. Therefore, considerable attention in the monitoring of contamination is required for food safety. Mycotoxins play an important role in food poisoning and trace amounts cause high toxicity in food samples. Various toxins, such as aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), deoxynivalenol (DON), fumonisin B1 (FB1), ochratoxin A (OTA), T-2 toxin (T-2), and zearalenone (ZEN), are present in food products. OTA is non-fluorescent in nature but emits blue fluorescence when an acid or alkaline solution is added; the best fluorescence result was obtained in an alkaline solution. A UV-LED in the range of 360–380 nm was used as a source to excite an OTA sample and an iPhone 4S was employed to capture the fluorescence image. Then, a graphical user interface (GUI) in a MATLAB R2011a program was used to obtain RGB values from the fluorescence image. The LOD using this method was 2 μg/L, which is comparable to a commercial kit (Bueno et al., 2016). Through rapid technology development, multiple mycotoxins can be detected simultaneously. Nanoparticle-based techniques have been involved for this purpose. Synthesized upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) consisting of three individual metals (yttrium, erbium, and ytterbium) were converted into immuno microspheres and attached with three different mycotoxins. Then, two laser lights (980 and 488 nm) were used to procure three fluorescence colors of the mycotoxins attached to the immuno microspheres. For quantification of mycotoxins, a self-made app was installed on a smartphone to convert the fluorescence images into a digital value using the HSV mode. In a real sample, 2 ng mL−1 of OTA, ZEN, and AFB1 in spiked corn had determined concentrations of 2.08, 1.77, and 2.44 ng mL−1, respectively (Yang et al., 2018).

Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) membranes can act as synthetic mimics of antibodies and enzymes, and have more stability than biosensors constructed using biomolecules. Interestingly, MIP provides more binding sites to capture toxic molecules, thus, AFB1 was efficiently attached to an MIP surface and emitted fluorescence in the presence of UV (Sergeyeva et al., 2019). The captured image was processed by the commercially available Spotxel® Reader app (Sicasys Software GmbH, Germany) for quantification of the AFB1. The LOD was found to be 20 ng mL−1, however, the detection sensitivity was lower than the UCNP-based method (Yang et al., 2018). In another method, shape-encoded hydrogel particles with a specific recognition toxin aptamer were synthesized for simultaneous detection of mycotoxins (Ji et al., 2019). A self-made Android app was used to convert the RGB image into a gray image for calculating the fluorescence intensity of the mycotoxins. Based on the fluorescence intensity, the detection limit of AFB1 and OTA reached 0.1 ng, which is a higher sensitivity than previously published methods (Sergeyeva et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2018).

The solid phase latex microsphere immunochromatography method was used for quantitative detection of ZEN. In this method, latex microsphere (LM) immunoconjugates were fabricated by mixing various chemicals, such as LMs, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and anti-ZEN mAb, in an appropriate volume, and was used as a target probe. Based on the quantity of ZEN in the sample, the color intensity of the target probe varied. A custom app was designed to extract the RGB values from the images and the LOD of ZEN in cereals was 0.08 μg kg−1 (Li et al., 2019c). Recently, Liu et al. used the lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) principle for the detection of various mycotoxins in a single experiment. In this process, a white LED and UV LED were used for visible and fluorescence detection modes. The Android Studio (version 3.3.1) platform was used to develop an app for obtaining RGB data from the test line color. The pixel value of green and blue channels was considered for visible detection and the pixel value of the red channel was selected for fluorescence detection modes. Furthermore, the LOD of AFB1, ZEN, DON, T-2, and FB1 were 10, 2.5, 1.0, 10, and 0.5 μg kg−1, respectively. Overall, in terms of sensitivity and simultaneous detection of mycotoxins, this method showed the advantage of LFIA (Fig. 3 ) (Liu et al., 2020b).

Foodborne pathogens are generally present in contaminated or uncooked food and produce severe health problems that have led to death on a few occasions. The most commonly recognized pathogens, such as Escherichia coli O157:H7, Salmonella enteritidis, Listeria monocytogenes, and Vibrio parahaemolyticus, are present in food. It is imperative to detect these pathogens by facile methods (Shrivastava et al., 2020). Nanozymes are an excellent class of materials employed as a signal amplifier in the pathogen detection process (Zhang et al., 2019b). Typically, nanozyme-attached pathogens perform a peroxidase-like activity in the presence of tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and produce an intense blue color (Zhang et al., 2016). Cheng et al. followed the LFIA technique using a Pd@Pt nanozyme for the simultaneous detection of E. coli and S. enteritidis. The LOD was calculated to be 34 colony-forming unit (CFU) mL−1 for E. coli and 20 CFU mL−1 for S. enteritidis using ImageJ software (Cheng et al., 2017). In another study, E. coli was detected by a fluorescent-based immunosensor technique. In the immunosensor, magnetic beads and FITC were labeled with appropriate antibodies, which were conjugated with target E. coli to form a sandwich structure. Using a blue laser light (405 nm), the sandwich structure yielded a fluorescent signal in the presence of the target pathogen. An iPhone 5S and ImageJ software were effectively involved in converting the fluorescence image into fluorescence intensity, which permits quantification of the E. coli. The LOD of E. coli in samples of yoghurt and egg were 1 CFU mL−1 and 10 CFU mL−1, respectively (Zeinhom et al., 2018b). Zheng et al. proposed a combination of the microfluidic chip, immunoassay, and smartphone for the detection of E. coli. In this method, the HSL-based imaging app was used to determine the E. coli concentration in chicken samples and the LOD was 50 CFU mL−1 (Zheng et al., 2019).

Zeinhom et al. prepared a sandwich immunosensor that contained magnetic beads and inorganic nanoflowers to rapidly detect S. enteritidis. Here the synthesized inorganic nanoflowers act peroxidase-like activity and produced blue color with the addition of TMB. RGB color values of the captured image were obtained through ImageJ software. The developed assay was able to detect S. enteritidis in milk and cheese with a LOD of 1 CFU mL−1 and 1 CFU/g, respectively (Fig. 4 ) (Zeinhom et al., 2018a). Moreover, the inorganic nanoflowers exhibited higher sensitivity than the Pd@Pt nanozyme for S. enteritidis detection (Cheng et al., 2017). Glucose oxidase (GOx) is a common enzyme used to convert glucose into H2O2 and gluconic acid in biosensors (Yang et al., 2013). Guo et al. have adopted this principle for the detection of S. typhimurium. In their process, immuno GOx-nanoclusters (GNCs) were synthesized and attached to S. typhimurium to produce nanocluster bacteria (GNCBs). The GNCBs were able to catalyze the glycose, which produced color in the strip. The image of the strip was collected and processed using a self-developed app. The hue, saturation, and lightness (HSL) color space platform was introduced for the quantification of S. typhimurium and to identify the LOD of 1.6 × 10 CFU mL−1 (Guo et al., 2019).

o-phenylenediamine (OPD) is a colorless substance that produces a pale yellow color and orange fluorescence in UV when it interacts with metal ions, such as Cu2+ and Ag+, due to the formation of 2,3- diaminophenazine (DAP) (Ma et al., 2017). However, Cu2+ is reduced to Cu+ in the presence of E. coli and, simultaneously, the color of the solution changes. This approach was established for rapid E. coli detection. With the help of a smartphone color-scanning app, color intensities were analyzed and quantified for E. coli. The LOD of E. coli using colorimetric and fluorescent strategies were 44 CFU mL−1 and 100 CFU mL−1, respectively (Wang et al., 2020). Moreover, this system has shown lower sensitivity compared with the nanozyme-based immunosensor (Zeinhom et al., 2018b). A pipette-actuated capillary array comb (PAAC) system has been successfully developed and operated on a smartphone-based hand-held device for the multiplex detection of bacteria. Using this method, the multiplex detection of genomic DNA extracted from E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and S. aureus provided limits of detection of 200, 500, and 500 copies, respectively (Hui et al., 2018).

Recently, a smartphone integrated genetic analyzer (i-Gene) was fabricated and utilized for monitoring a real-time loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) reaction. When the LAMP proceeded, the violet color (EBT-Mg complex) changed to blue color and the color intensity of the captured image was processed using ImageJ software. Multiplex foodborne pathogens, such as E. coli, S. typhimurium, and V. parahaemolyticus, were simultaneously identified using this method and the LOD of E. coli was 101 copies μL−1 (Nguyen et al., 2020b). Interestingly, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) approach was also successful for E. coli detection. Graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposites, which dramatically stabilize the ECL emission owing to their higher catalytic activity and amplification efficiency towards luminophores, were synthesized in this process. GQD nanocomposites amplified the ECL of luminophores in the presence of the E. coli antibody, which was immobilized on the electrode surface. The luminescence signals were processed by a smartphone-based image analysis app that exhibited a good linear range of 10–107 CFU mL−1 towards the detection of E. coli (Liu et al., 2020b).

Herbicide and insecticide have been widely used to protect crops against weeds, fungi, insects, and other pests. However, due to the excessive use of pesticides for higher crop production, pesticide residues remain in food products, leading to health-related problems in humans and animals. Thus, advanced technologies for pesticide detection are in high demand (Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020). Mei et al. have developed a fluorescence-based strategy for the detection of the pesticide thiram. Initially, UCNPs were synthesized and decorated with copper ions (Cu2+) to obtain upconversion nanoprobes (Cu@UCNPs). The Cu@UCNPs were coated on filter paper and emitted blue luminescence when laser light (980 nm) was applied. Subsequently, the addition of thiram quenched the blue luminescence through the resonance energy transfer process. These intensity variations were monitored by a self-written app and the blue channel intensities were extracted from RGB channel values to quantify the thiram. The LOD was 0.1 μM and when 50 μM of thiram was injected into apple juice, it was detected at 55.32 μM with a recovery of 110.64% (Mei et al., 2016).

Nanozyme-based biosensors were effectively utilized in the LFA method for pesticide detection (Cheng et al., 2017; Zeinhom et al., 2018a) and Pt@Ni-NSs nanosheets were synthesized to act as a signal amplifier due to their peroxidase-like activity. A self-made app, named Strip Scan, was employed for the readout of the results. Acetochlor and fenpropathrin were simultaneously detected and their LOD values were 0.63 ng mL−1 and 0.24 ng mL−1, respectively (Fig. 5 ) (Cheng et al., 2019). Jin et al. have developed a ratiometric fluorescent method for paraoxon detection using OPD and silicon quantum dots (SiQDs). In their process, Ag+ ions play an important role wherein they trigger the oxidation of OPD and produce DAP with stronger emission in the presence of paraoxon (Ma et al., 2017). Apparently, the emission of SiQDs is quenched further due to the inner filter effect and these ratiometric fluorescence signals were converted into the hue parameter using ImageJ software to quantify the concentration of the paraoxon. The LOD was 10 ng mL−1 (Jin et al., 2019).

The advantages of the ratiometric fluorescent process and the unprecedented role of QDs were considered for pesticide detection (Jin et al., 2019). CdTe QDs were attached inside silica nanoparticles (SiNPs) and carbon dots (CDs), acting as a signal transducer, were embedded on the outer surface of the SiNPs. The blue fluorescence of the CDs was quenched by the addition of AuNPs due to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and AuNPs. However, blue fluorescence reappeared when thiram was added because AuNPs preferred to attach to the sulfur group of the thiram. A color recognizer app processed the RGB channel values of the captured images and the ratio of the R/B channel was used for thiram quantification (Chu et al., 2020). The determined LOD was 59 nM, which is a higher sensitivity than the UCNP-based method (Mei et al., 2016). In general, AuNPs have shown two types of colors, i.e., red color that represents particle dispersion and blue color that represents particle aggregated. These AuNP properties could help to detect pesticides in real samples. Citrate-capped AuNPs were further modified with Tween-20 to increase their stability in different pH (5.5–8.0). However, with the addition of cyromazine, the AuNP stability collapsed, which resulted in blue color. A self-written app was developed to quantify the cyromazine and the LOD was 0.04 mg kg−1 (Liu et al., 2020a).

Methyl parathion (MP) has been monitored based on cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) capped CdTe QDs and CD nanoprobes. The emission of CTAB capped CdTe QDs is quenched in the presence of MP, however, CD emission remains constant. Because the anionic form of MP could easily access the QD surface, due to the positive charge of the CTAB, it quenched the fluorescence intensity of the QDs through electron transfer. Furthermore, the HSL color model has been employed for analyzing color changes for quantification of MP; the LOD was 0.06 μg/mL (Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020). Recently, Jing et al. have reported an azo dye-based technique for the effective detection of carbaryl. In this method, carbaryl was initially converted into 1-naphthol, which was coupled with 4-methoxybenzenediazonium tetrafluoroborate and produced red color azo dye. A microfluidic platform was utilized for this method to detect carbaryl in the form of azo dye. Image processing software (Adobe Photoshop CC, USA) was employed to obtain the RGB values of the color and the LOD of carbaryl was 0.027 mg/kg (Jing et al., 2021).

QD and nanozyme sensors actively participated in paraoxon detection by ratiometric fluorescent and mimic oxidase activity methods (Jin et al., 2019, 2021). In the peroxidase activity process, MnO2 nanoflakes (MnNFs) were synthesized and their activity was affected by the addition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE). However, the peroxidase activity of MnNFs was triggered in the presence of paraoxon because paraoxon is an inhibitor of AChE (Jin et al., 2021). Using a self-made app, optical images were converted into gray value for quantification of paraoxon and the LOD was 0.5 ng mL−1, which is a higher sensitivity than the hybrid hydrogels-based method (Jin et al., 2019).

Food preservation is a crucial technique enabling food products to be maintained in safe conditions for long periods of time. The addition of food additives is one way to preserve food and CH2O is most commonly used for this purpose. However, excessive quantities of CH2O result in adverse health-related problems in humans. Smartphone-based detection methods have been utilized to detect CH2O in food products. Guzman et al. have proposed a microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD) for the detection of CH2O, even at low concentrations. The CH2O produces a complex with acetoacetanilide in the presence of ammonium acetate and the color that appeared in the paper was captured by a smartphone camera. The images were converted into an RGB color model using a self-driven app and the blue channel intensity had a linear correlation with concentrations of CH2O in the range of 0.2–2.5 ppm. The LOD of this method (0.2 ppm) was much lower than the standard spectrophotometric method (2 ppm) (Guzman et al., 2018).

H2O2 is often utilized in food processing sectors due to its significant biological degradability and chemical properties. It is also used as an antimicrobial agent in milk and as a sterilizing agent for food packaging materials. Moreover, H2O2 has strong oxidizing properties and used as a bleaching agent in some foods such as edible oil, wheat flour, egg white, etc. However, excess levels of H2O2 in food may cause mild irritation and vomiting. Peng et al. have introduced a peroxidase-like activity technique for the detection of H2O2. For this purpose, B, N, and S-doped carbon dots (BNS-CDs) were synthesized and act as nanozymes, producing blue color by converting TMB to ox-TAB in the presence of H2O2, as in pathogen detection (Zhang et al., 2016). Android Color Grab was used to obtain RGB values of the blue color image and the LOD was determined to be 0.8 μM (Peng et al., 2020).

All the above discussed smartphone-based techniques involved in detecting various food contaminants, such as antibiotics, enzymes, mycotoxins, pathogens, pesticides, and small molecules, are shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based techniques for metal ion detection in environmental water

ce:section.ce:para: The chromium ion has two oxidation states: hexavalent chromium ion (Cr6+), which is highly toxic, and trivalent chromium ion (Cr3+), which is less toxic and used as a micronutrient for humans. However, high levels of Cr3+ can bind with DNA and affect some cellular tissue in living organisms. Therefore, the development of sensitive techniques for the detection of Cr3+ ions is necessary. Yu et al. have developed an ELISA-based technique for the detection of Cr3+ ions using a new portable device named the “Light Meter.” In their work, the fabricated immunosensor actively participated in peroxidase-like activity and produced a blue color in the solution. The Light Meter contained a smartphone with an app that converted the color into light intensity to quantify the metal ions. Using this method, the LOD was calculated to be 0.81 ng mL−1 (Yu et al., 2016).

The AuNP aggregation strategy is another method for Cr3+ ion detection. In this method, pyridoxal 5′-phosphate (PLP) and cysteamine (CA) react to form a Schiff base (CAPLP), which is utilized to decorate the surface of AuNPs. Excitingly, the Cr3+ ions selectively induced aggregation of the CAPLP-capped AuNPs and other metal ions sustained the original red color of the CAPLP-capped AuNPs. Furthermore, an RGB color value app in the iPhone 6s processed the color image and the ratio of R/G was considered to determine the Cr3+ level; the LOD was 11 μM (Upadhyay et al., 2018b). Recently, Shalaby et al. have reported a simple method for the detection of Cr6+ ions. In this method, diphenylcarbazide is the colorless solid used as a substrate material that developed a violet color when reacting with Cr6+ ions. The color image was processed into RGB values using ImageJ software and the LOD of Cr6+ ions was 0.01 μg mL−1 (Shalaby and Mohamed, 2020).

The Cu2+ ion is an essential metal ion that promotes biological functions when at the appropriate concentration in living organisms. Excess or a deficient quantity of Cu2+ ions causes many diseases in humans. Red beet pigment is a natural food pigment that was preferred as a colorimetric indicator for the detection of Cu2+ ions. Red beet pigment reacted selectively with Cu2+ under alkaline conditions resulting in a color change from purple to orange-red. A self-written app was developed to calculate the RGB values and observed a good linear relationship between B channel value and the concentration of Cu2+ ion in the range of 4–20 μM, with a detection limit of 0.84 μM (Cao et al., 2019).

A chemically synthesized spiropyran compound was effectively utilized for the detection of Cu2+ ions. The spiropyran compound selectively produced a distinct pink color by making a complex with Cu2+ ions while other metal ions did not develop color on the paper strips. The LOD of Cu2+ in the paper strip was 0.61 μM and this sensor was capable of detecting beyond the level of the World Health Organization (WHO)-recommended Cu2+ ion limit in water (20 μM) (Kumar et al., 2020). The Shen group designed an excited-state intramolecular proton transfer-based sensor for Cu2+ ion detection and N-linked disalicylaldehyde (N-DSA) was synthesized for this purpose. In the method, the N-DSA made a complex with the Cu2+ ion, which affected the excited state electron transfer and the fluorescence of the N-DNA was quenched. The Color Assist app obtained the RGB value of the image and the ratio of the blue and RGB channel (B/RGB) showed a linear correlation against the concentrations of Cu2+ ions in the range of 0–5 mM. The LOD was determined to be 0.12 mM (Fig. 6 ) (Shen and Zhang, 2021).

In drinking water, the F− ion is a highly toxic element and high content causes kidney failure, dental fluorosis, and/or DNA damage. Hence, emerging smartphone-based techniques provide promising methods for F− ion detection. A simple zirconyl-4,5-dihydroxy-3-(4-sulfophenylazo)-2,7-naphthalene disulfonic acid, trisodium salt (SPADNS) colorimetric method is generally used for F− ion determination. Vidal et al. followed this strategy, combined with a μPAD, for detecting F− ions using smartphone technology. ImageJ software was used to convert the RGB color space from the digital image. Moreover, the value of the green channel showed a linear correlation with F− concentration in the range of 0.23–2.26 mg L−1; the LOD was 0.25 mg L−1 (Vidal et al., 2018). Hybrid QDs have produced a ratiometric fluorescence strategy for pesticide detection (Jin et al., 2019; Chu et al., 2020) and, similarly, an amino-modified QD (NH2@QDs) nanohybrid was applied for F− ion identification. In this process, the 2-(tert-butyldiphenylsilyloxy)phenol was converted into o-quinone in the presence of F− ions and the o-quinone quenched the green fluorescence of the NH2@QDs when reacted. The Color Picker app was used for obtaining RGB values and the ratio of red and green channels (R/G) showed an excellent linear relationship to F− concentration in the range of 0–70 μM (Fig. 7 ) (Zhang et al., 2019a). The LOD was calculated to be 2 μM and this sensor detected much lower values than the permissible level of F− ions (63.16 μM) in drinking water, as recommended by the WHO.

Apart from QDs and CDs (Chu et al., 2020; Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020), mixed lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) have been an emerging method for constructing effective ratiometric fluorescent sensors (Wu et al., 2020). In Ln-MOFs, the lanthanide acts as cations able to bind to the F− anion, which affected the luminescence of the Ln-MOFs. Furthermore, the green luminescence of Ln-MOFs was transformed to red with increasing F− ion concentration. A color extraction app was used for color information and the ratio of green and red channels (G/R) were selected to calculate the F− ion concentration (Zeng et al., 2020). Recently, Mukherjee et al. have developed core-shell nanocages (NCs) with a xylenol orange (XO)-based sensor for quantification of F− ions at sub-ppm levels. XO is a signal indicator and its original color turns red in the presence of F− ions. A self-driven Android app was created, using the MIT app Inventor 2 program, to monitor color changes. This sensor showed excellent sensitivity to F− ions in the range of 0.1–5 ppm with a LOD of 0.06 ppm (Mukherjee et al., 2020).

The Fe3+ ion is an important element that stimulates many biological processes in the human body. Fe3+ deficiency promotes fatigue and anemia, whereas high accumulation causes renal failure. Thus, rapid and sensitive determination of Fe3+ ions is a high priority. A non-fluorescent pyrene-based sensor was chemically synthesized and exhibited yellow color in solution. During interaction with Fe3+ ions, the sensor’s yellow color became colorless and blue emission was generated. A Color Assist app (Shen and Zhang, 2021) was developed to calculate the RGB color intensity and the intensity ratio of the red and blue color channel (R/B) showed a good linear correlation with Fe3+ concentration in the range of 5–34 μM (Upadhyay et al., 2018a). Another method followed the simple colorimetric principle for the detection of Fe3+ ions. In this work, Fe3+ ions were mixed with 1,10-phenanthroline to develop the color and ImageJ software was used to convert the RGB intensity of the obtained image. In a real sample, the Fe3+ ion concentration was estimated to be 0.69 μg mL−1 and this detection limit almost matched that of the spectrophotometry method (Mohamed and Shalaby, 2019). Similarly, Li et al. followed the 1,10-phenanthroline strategy (Mohamed and Shalaby, 2019) combined with μPAD for Fe3+ ion quantification. The average gray-scale of RGB color intensities showed a linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in a range from 0.3 to 18 mg L−1 (Li et al., 2019a).

As metal ions quenched the fluorescence of Ln-MOF (Zeng et al., 2020), likewise, hydroxy functionalized MOFs (OH@MOFs) were synthesized and their fluorescence intensity was quenched due to the interaction between the Fe3+ ion and hydroxyl group of the MOF. A smartphone-based fluorescence reader was used to monitor the changes of the fluorescence signal and to calculate the Fe3+ ion concentration. Using this method, the linear detection range of the Fe3+ ion was 5–200 μM and the LOD was 1.7 μM (Zhao et al., 2019).

Due to high toxicity, Pb2+ ions can damage human organs and tissues even at low concentrations with larger quantities causing cancer in humans. Recent optical and biological sensors have been involved with tremendous effects for the determination of Pb2+ ions. Nguyen et al. have developed a dark-field smartphone microscope-based technique to quantify Pb2+ ions at the nanoscale level. In this process, a highly insoluble lead chromate (PdCrO4) solid was synthesized by reacting Pb2+ and chromate ions. Specifically, the microscopic yellow pixel intensity of the PdCrO4 had a good linear relationship with Pb2+ concentration in the range of 5–175 ppb. The entire detection process was completed in less than 10 min, thus, making it quite convenient for on-site detection (Nguyen et al., 2018). In another method, red and blue fluorescence CDs were synthesized and involved in a ratiometric fluorescent strategy for Pb2+ ion detection. The blue fluorescence was quenched in the paper strips, due to the IFE (Han et al., 2020) between the Pb2+ ions and blue CDs, and the color information (RGB values) was obtained by a color recognizer app. The ratio of blue and red channels (B/R) was directly proportional to the concentration of Pb2+ ions, within the range of 15–80 nM, and the LOD was 45.86 nM (Wang et al., 2019a).

Owing to technological advancement, the simultaneous detection of metal ions is preferred and Xiao et al. have designed a novel technique to find Hg2+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ ions in a single experiment (Xiao et al., 2020). In this method, three types of CDs were synthesized, based on the binding properties of the metal ions, and the fluorescence turn-off strategy was applied for quantification. A Java-based app was designed to extract the RGB values from the fluorescence images, which converted into grayscale values for metal ion detection. Using this method, the LOD of Pb2+ ions was 0.078 μM in a real sample, which is comparable with the result of ICP-MS. Moreover, the LOD of Cu2+ ions was 0.38 μM, which is higher sensitivity than the spiropyran-based method (Kumar et al., 2020). Essentially, the AuNP surface is modified with oligonucleotides, aptamers, and DNases capable of binding to the metal ions due to the presence of nitrogen, carboxyl groups, and sulfhydryl groups. Thus, Sajed et al. have constructed oligonucleotide functionalized AuNPs for the detection of Pb2+ ions. Typically, the oligonucleotide functionalized AuNPs color changed to blue when Pb2+ ions were present. This sensor showed better performance in real samples, with an average recovery percentage of 93.7% (Sajed et al., 2020). Similarly, AgNPs were also involved in Pb2+ ion detection based on their surface plasmon resonance (SPR) properties. The color of AgNPs changed from yellow to red in the presence of Pb2+ ions and ImageJ software was used to convert the RGB values of the digital images; the LOD of Pb2+ was 1.5 ppm (Emmanuel et al., 2020). Apart from colorimetric and fluorescent-based sensors, an electrochemical sensor was also developed for Pb2+ ion detection. A hand-held detector was designed and connected to a smartphone, which detected Pb2+ ion concentration as low as 45 nM using a square wave voltammetry-based technique (Liao et al., 2020).

Mercury is a primary element of water pollution that can be converted into hypertoxic organic mercury by aquatic microorganisms. Even trace levels of Hg2+ ions results in severe problems to human organs, such as the heart, central nervous system, and kidney. Thus, emerging smartphone-based techniques have been utilized for highly accurate and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions. The AuNP aggregation strategy in the presence of metal ions (Sajed et al., 2020) has been involved in Hg2+ ion detection. A self-developed app was used to quantify the colorimetric results and the LOD of Hg2+ ions was 10.77 μg/L (Motalebizadeh et al., 2018). UCNPs are commonly used as signal amplifiers due to their fluorescence behavior and their quantum of contribution in the field of mycotoxin and pesticide detection (Yang et al., 2018; Mei et al., 2016). Jin et al. have synthesized red fluorescence UCNPs, applied using the LFA technique, for the detection of Hg2+ ions. ImageJ software obtained the average gray values of the digital images and a linear relationship with Hg2+ ion concentration was observed in the range of 10–104 ppb; the LOD of Hg2+ ions was 5 ppb (Jin et al., 2018).

Nanozymes commonly acted as a peroxidase-like activity that converted TMB into oxTMB and produced blue color (Zhang et al., 2019b). However, chitosan-functionalized nanozymes did not initiate the oxidation of TMB. The nitrogen in the chitosan has the ability to adsorb Hg2+ ions (Miretzky and Cirelli, 2009). Then, the oxidation proceeded successfully and developed the blue color in the solution. With the help of the Color Assist app, the observed LOD of the Hg2+ ion was 27 nM (Huang et al., 2019). Xiao et al. have designed the “Light Meter” to detect Cr3+ ions and the same phenomenon was introduced for Hg2+ ion detection using an aptamer functionalized AuNPs sensor; the calculated LOD of Hg2+ was 0.28 ng mL−1 (Xiao et al., 2016). The SPR of AgNPs changes when the NPs interact with metal ions and higher concentrations of Hg2+ ions produced a colorless AgNPs solution due to the redox reaction. These color differences were exploited for detecting the Hg2+ ion using the “Mercury Detector” app, available on the Google Play Store, and the LOD of the Hg2+ ion was 0.86 ppb (Firdaus et al., 2019).

Magnetic bead-based fluorescent biosensors selectively capture Hg2+ ions and the obtained fluorescence signals were collected by a smartphone fluorescence microscope. The constructed image processing app produced a linear correlation with Hg2+ ions in the range between 1 nM and 1 μM (Shan et al., 2019). Another microscope technique has been developed to capture nanoscale sediment of red mercury iodide by calculating the intensity of the images to determine the concentration of Hg2+ ions. Using this method, the LOD was calculated to be 2 ppb (Nguyen et al., 2020a). In this method, HSV color space and grayscale intensity calculations were used to determine the concentration of Hg2+ ions and this same technique was adopted for Pb2+ ion detection (Nguyen et al., 2018). Liu et al. have established a CdSe/ZnS QD-based fluorescent sensor for detecting Hg2+ ions. Initially, carboxyl terminated QDs, which make more binding sites for Hg2+ ions, were synthesized and fluorescence quenching was monitored during the addition of Hg2+. A self-developed app was involved for accurate quantification and the concentration ranging from 1 to 1000 nM had a linear correlation with the fluorescence intensities (Fig. 8 ) (Liu et al., 2019).

Rhodamine6G (R6G) is a commercially available fluorescent compound and it forms a complex with Hg2+ ions that substantially affected the fluorescence emission properties of R6G. With increasing Hg2+ ion concentration, the fluorescence intensity of R6G was gradually quenched and a smartphone sensor could easily detect this variation. A custom-made app was designed to convert the fluorescence signal into readable data and this sensor was capable of detecting Hg2+ as low as 32 ppb (Hatiboruah et al., 2020). Similar to the advantages of colorimetric and fluorescent sensors, the smartphone-based electrochemical technique also provides a moderate contribution to metal ion detection. Recently, Xu et al. have established a customized carbon electrode for the simultaneous detection of various metal ions, such as Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+, using differential pulse voltammetry measurements. A self-written Android app, DHMI, was designed for quantification and LOD determination of Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+, which were calculated to be 0.29, 0.05, 0.35, and 0.02 μM, respectively (Xu et al., 2020).

The aluminum ion (Al3+) has been detected using a CD-based fluorescent sensor established by Rao et al. The synthesized CDs showed blue and red emission and the Al3+ ion particularly induced the blue emission. The fluorescence variations were captured by a self-designed app named “Fitting detection”. Furthermore, the concentration of Al3+ ions had a linear relationship with the blue channel value of RGB channels in the range of 6–70 μM. In a real sample, 1.8 μM of Al3+ ion was injected in water and detected at 2.11 μM with a recovery of 100.02% (Rao et al., 2020). The aptamer-modified AuNPs enable the detection of cadmium ions (Cd2+) based on the aggregation principle of AuNPs (Sajed et al., 2020). A self-developed app was employed to get the RGB value and the ratio of red and blue channel value (R/B) was considered for quantification of Cd2+. This method was highly selective towards Cd2+ ions and the LOD was 1.12 μg L−1 (Fig. 9 ) (Gan et al., 2020). Peng et al. have introduced the dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction method to determine trace calcium ions (Ca2+) in water. In this process, Ca2+ ions reacted with glyoxal bis(2-Hydroxyanil) (GBHA) to produce intense red-colored Ca-GBHA complex. The Color Grab app was used for collecting the RGB values of the color complex. The green channel (G) exhibited a good linear response with Ca2+ ion concentration in the range of 0.06–1.5 μg mL−1 (Peng et al., 2019).

The Chu group has demonstrated μPAD techniques for heavy metal ion detection using triple colorimetric indicators. Initially, the triple indicators, such as (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane, 1,2-Phenylenediamine, and tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin, were preloaded on the μPAD and resulted in three different colors with the addition of nickel ions (Ni2+). Using this sensor, the LOD of Ni2+ was 0.5 μM, and various metal ions, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Pb2+, have also been detected by this method (Idros and Chu, 2018). Dithizone is a chromogenic reagent that effectively chelates the zinc ion (Zn2+). The color of the dithizone changes from green to purple by producing the Zn-dithizone complex. The Light Meter app was used to monitor the intensity of the color and the LOD of Zn2+ ion was 0.02 μg mL−1 (Li et al., 2020).
All the above mentioned smartphone-based techniques involved in detecting various metal ions, such as chromium, copper, fluoride, iron, lead, and mercury, are listed in Table 2 .

Many published articles in the field of food safety and metal ion detection, using different transduction principles integrated with smartphone technology, have been summarized in this review. While many of these studies were only utilized for proof-of-concept purposes, they could be converted into commercial devices for real applications in the near future. From Tables 1 and 2, the colorimetric sensor based on aptamer and oligonucleotide labeled AuNPs were introduced for the detection of antibiotics, pesticides, and metal ions, and produced an excellent detection limit. Likewise, colorimetric immunosensors using LFIA and microarray techniques were involved in the quantification of antibiotics, ALP, and mycotoxins. Despite the many advantages of these methods, the utilization of an aptamer, oligonucleotide, and antibodies in the fabrication of colorimetric sensors could likely interfere and limit their futuristic applications. This is because the functionalization of the aptamer and oligonucleotide with AuNPs is a relatively complicated process. Additionally, the production of antibodies is expensive and animals are used for this purpose, which causes ethical issues. Notably, the higher stability of the sensors provides more benefits because long shelf-life of sensors in atmospheric conditions do not require sophisticated equipment for storage, thus, they can be used for smartphone POCTs in resource-limited settings. However, aptamer, oligonucleotide, and antibody-labeled sensors are significantly affected by atmospheric temperature and humidity, consequently reduced their stability.

QD-based fluorescence sensors were utilized to detect antibiotics, pesticides, and metal ions due to their distinctive optical properties. They produced highly accurate results based on the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. QD-based fluorescence sensors were highly stable, even at room temperature. However, this method has a few limitations, particularly, the mechanism of the photoluminescence properties is still unclear. Nanozymes are artificial enzymes applied in pathogen, pesticide, and metal ion detection processes. In terms of cost, preparation methods, surface modification, and stability, nanozymes are superior to biological enzymes. However, their catalytic activity is relatively lower than that of biological enzymes. To overcome this, integrating nanozymes into mesoporous materials such as hydrogels, mesoporous carbon, and MOFs could be an auspicious strategy to achieve highly active nanozymes.

Based on this review, smartphones have been extensively used as signal detectors and data processors due to their intrinsic features, which make them a promising source for analyzing various target analytes in food and water samples in real-time. Owing to the touch screen, wireless connectivity, light sensors, CMOS camera, on-site diagnosis, and portability, smartphone-based POCTs have an incredible advantage over conventional methods. Moreover, an advancement in materials science is still required for the production of new organic and inorganic substances that overcome limitations remaining in optical sensors (stability, user-friendliness, etc.). Such sensor developments will surely increase the practicability of these technologies to efficiently monitor food safety and water quality, particularly in resource-limited regions.

Rajamanickam Sivakumar: Conceptualization; Investigation; Data curation; Writing – original draft. Nae Yoon Lee: Resources; Supervision; Project administration; Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
